items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Variability of Sarcoidosis Incidence
items.content: Sarcoidosis incidence varies significantly by race, gender, geographic region, ethnicity, and season. African Americans have the highest reported incidence globally at 35.5 per 100,000, followed by Northern Europeans at 24 per 100,000. The disease is more common in women than men but does not exceed a 2:1 ratio. Peak incidence occurs between ages 35-45, with an underestimation of overall rates due to subclinical cases.
keyPoints: Highest incidence in African Americans (35.5 per 100,000) and Northern Europeans (24 per 100,000).
Higher incidence in women across most studies; ratio does not exceed 2:1.
Peak incidence between ages 35-45 years.
Potential underestimation of overall incidence due to subclinical cases.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Regional and Demographic Variability in Sarcoidosis Presentation
items.content: The presentation of sarcoidosis varies by region and demographic factors. Cardiac and ocular involvement are more common in Japanese populations, while erythema nodosum is prevalent among Europeans but rare in Japanese and black populations. Black patients often experience more severe extrapulmonary disease and have a higher likelihood of familial disease.
keyPoints: Cardiac and ocular sarcoidosis more common in Japanese populations.
Erythema nodosum more frequent in European populations, rare in Japanese and blacks.
Black patients show greater severity with extrapulmonary involvement and 3x likelihood of familial disease.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Impact of Socioeconomic Status on Sarcoidosis Severity
items.content: Lower socioeconomic status is associated with increased severity and disability in sarcoidosis patients. This includes greater dyspnea, new organ involvement, and overall impairment, suggesting that socioeconomic factors may influence disease course.
keyPoints: Lower income linked to higher severity and disability in sarcoidosis.
Increased dyspnea and onset of new organ involvement associated with lower socioeconomic status.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Understanding Mortality Variability in Sarcoidosis
items.content: Mortality rates from sarcoidosis vary based on the study setting, with lower mortality noted in population screenings compared to referral centers. This reflects differences in disease severity and patient demographics.
keyPoints: Average age- and gender-adjusted mortality rate in the U.S.: 4.32 per 1,000,000
Higher mortality rates observed in women and African Americans
Pulmonary fibrosis is the leading cause of death
Lower mortality rates in Japan: 0.1 per million
Cardiac involvement is a common cause of death in Japan, while respiratory causes predominate in Europeans and Americans

items.title: Etiology of Sarcoidosis: Immune Response and Genetic Susceptibility
items.content: The cause of sarcoidosis is linked to a heightened immune response to an unknown environmental antigen in genetically susceptible individuals. The disease may involve multiple antigens, with evidence from immunologic studies.
keyPoints: Sarcoidosis involves exaggerated or deficient regulatory mechanisms in the immune response
Kveim-Siltzbach reagent induces systemic granulomatous response in early sarcoidosis patients
Mycobacterial proteins found disproportionately in sarcoidosis granulomas
Genetic predisposition indicated by antigen recognition and presentation molecule polymorphisms

items.title: Environmental Exposures and Occupational Risks Associated with Sarcoidosis
items.content: Sarcoidosis incidence is linked to environmental exposures, with occupational risks highlighted by case-cluster events. Certain jobs have higher sarcoidosis rates due to specific exposures.
keyPoints: Higher incidence in U.S. firefighters: 13–15 per 100,000 annually
Post-World Trade Center event sarcoid-like disease rates: 86 per 100,000 initially, 22 per 100,000 over four years
Occupations linked to increased risk include agriculture, teaching, metalworking, and exposure to musty environments

items.title: Understanding the Immunopathogenesis and Clinical Presentation of Sarcoidosis
items.content: Sarcoidosis is characterized by granulomatous inflammation driven by a Type 1 immune response, involving CD4+ T_H1 lymphocytes producing IFN-γ. This process is marked by upregulation of specific cytokines and chemokines that amplify the inflammatory response. Clinically, sarcoidosis can present variably from asymptomatic to severe organ dysfunction, with acute presentations like Löfgren syndrome having a good prognosis.
keyPoints: Type 1 immune polarization is central in sarcoidosis pathogenesis, characterized by CD4+ T_H1 lymphocytes and IFN-γ production.
Cytokines such as IL-1, IL-2, IL-15, IL-18, and CXCR3 are upregulated at inflammation sites.
Acute-onset presentation in Löfgren syndrome occurs in up to 10% of cases with symptoms like fever, erythema nodosum, ankle arthralgias, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.
Chronic presentations may include cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or joint/muscle pain.
Severe manifestations can lead to hospitalization due to acute hypercalcemia, blindness, arrhythmias, heart block, or neurologic issues.

items.title: Key Considerations in Sarcoidosis Diagnosis and Management
items.content: The diagnosis of sarcoidosis can be challenging due to its variable clinical presentation, often mimicking more common diseases. Acute presentations like Löfgren syndrome have a favorable prognosis with spontaneous resolution, while chronic cases may lead to significant morbidity or mortality from organ involvement. Understanding the immunopathogenesis helps in recognizing potential complications and guiding treatment.
keyPoints: Sarcoidosis granulomas form around poorly degradable material presented by dendritic cells or macrophages.
Löfgren syndrome, seen in up to 10% of cases, has an excellent prognosis with full spontaneous resolution.
Chronic sarcoidosis can lead to organ dysfunction and severe complications such as heart failure or neurologic issues.
Symptoms like cough, chest pain, or shortness of breath may delay diagnosis due to similarity with common diseases.
Incidental findings on imaging often prompt further investigation into sarcoidosis.

items.title: When to Consider Biopsy in Suspected Sarcoidosis
items.content: Biopsy should be considered if a compatible clinical picture or radiographic finding suggests sarcoidosis. Noninvasive biopsy sites include skin, conjunctiva, or superficial lymph nodes. For lung involvement with mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy, bronchoscopy is preferred due to its high diagnostic yield and low risk. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided lymph node sampling can be used for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Biopsy considered when clinical/radiographic findings suggest sarcoidosis
Noninvasive biopsy sites: skin, conjunctiva, superficial lymph nodes
Bronchoscopy preferred for lung involvement with mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy
Endobronchial ultrasound-guided sampling has high diagnostic yield and low risk

items.title: Diagnostic Thresholds and Imaging in Sarcoidosis
items.content: A CD4:CD8 lymphocyte ratio >3.5 supports a diagnosis of sarcoidosis, especially with consistent clinical and radiographic findings. However, a normal ratio does not exclude the disease. BAL studies are crucial to exclude infection. $^{18}$F-FDG PET-CT can identify active inflammation and biopsy sites in challenging cases.
keyPoints: CD4:CD8 lymphocyte ratio >3.5 supports sarcoidosis diagnosis
Normal CD4:CD8 ratio does not exclude sarcoidosis
BAL studies important to exclude infection
$^{18}$F-FDG PET-CT helps identify active inflammation and biopsy sites

items.title: Understanding Sarcoidosis Granulomas and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: Sarcoidosis granulomas are nonnecrotizing collections of macrophages, epithelioid cells, multinucleated giant cells, surrounded by lymphocytes and fibroblasts. The presence of abundant necrosis suggests alternative diagnoses like infections or hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
keyPoints: Sarcoidosis granulomas are nonnecrotizing with rare punctuate necrosis.
Abundant necrosis indicates other causes such as infection.
Loosely formed nonnecrotizing granulomas suggest hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
Diagnosis relies on exclusion of other granulomatous diseases.

items.title: Initial Diagnostic Evaluation for Sarcoidosis
items.content: The initial evaluation includes a complete history and physical exam, assessing organ involvement. Key tests include pulmonary function tests, chest imaging, ophthalmologic investigation, ECG, and serum tests for calcium metabolism and organ function.
keyPoints: About half of sarcoidosis patients have extrapulmonary involvement.
Baseline testing includes pulmonary function tests, chest imaging, ophthalmologic evaluation, and ECG.
Serum tests should assess calcium metabolism and hepatic, renal, or hematologic involvement.
Hematologic abnormalities may be due to T cell redistribution, splenic sequestration, bone marrow involvement, or immune mediation.

items.title: Pulmonary Function Tests and Imaging in Sarcoidosis
items.content: In sarcoidosis, pulmonary function tests are crucial for assessing initial lung impairment and monitoring changes over time. Despite granulomatous inflammation and abnormal imaging findings, many patients may present with normal lung function or physical examination results.
keyPoints: Lungs involved in >90% of sarcoidosis cases.
Restrictive pattern often seen in advanced disease.
Airflow obstruction can occur in up to one-third of patients.
Chest x-rays used for staging (Scadding stages).

CT scans show nodules with bronchovascular and subpleural distribution, thickened interlobular septa, mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy, conglomerate masses, honeycombing, cyst/cavity formation, and bronchiectasis in advanced cases.
Nodular sarcoidosis or necrotizing sarcoid granulomatosis presents with nodular masses.

items.title: Complications of Progressive Pulmonary Fibrosis in Sarcoidosis
items.content: Progressive pulmonary fibrosis is a major complication in sarcoidosis, leading to irreversible scarring and potentially severe outcomes. It can result in cavitary lung disease, chronic pulmonary aspergillosis or aspergillomas, and pulmonary hypertension.
keyPoints: Fibrosis leads to complications like cavitary lung disease, chronic pulmonary aspergillosis/aspergillomas, and pulmonary hypertension.
Airflow obstruction due to central airway scarring and lung structure distortion.
Fibrosis can occur even with anti-inflammatory therapy.

items.title: Cardiac Sarcoidosis: A Silent Threat with Potentially Fatal Outcomes
items.content: Cardiac sarcoidosis can lead to severe complications such as sudden death, rhythm abnormalities, and heart failure. Despite its potential severity, clinical evidence of cardiac involvement is found in less than 5% of U.S. patients but significantly higher at 23% among Japanese patients with known sarcoidosis.
keyPoints: Clinical evidence of cardiac involvement: <5% in U.S., 23% in Japan
Sudden death as the most common cause of mortality
Ventricular tachycardia is the most frequent arrhythmia

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Imaging Techniques for Cardiac Sarcoidosis
items.content: Diagnosing cardiac sarcoidosis is challenging due to the patchy nature of granulomas. ECG should be performed initially, with further evaluation using Holter monitors or advanced imaging like enhanced cardiac MRI if abnormalities are detected.
keyPoints: ECG reveals conduction block, ST-T wave changes, Q waves
Enhanced cardiac MRI preferred for diagnosis due to high sensitivity and specificity
Cardiac PET/CT can detect active inflammation when MRI is unavailable

items.title: Management of Cardiac Sarcoidosis: From Diagnosis to Treatment
items.content: Management includes traditional treatments for arrhythmias and heart failure, along with immunosuppressive therapy. In severe cases, heart transplantation may be considered. Serial imaging helps guide the duration of therapy.
keyPoints: Immunosuppressive medications are part of treatment
Heart transplantation can improve survival in refractory cases
Serial imaging guides therapy duration

items.title: Identifying Pulmonary Hypertension in Sarcoidosis
items.content: In sarcoidosis, pulmonary hypertension (PH) is a significant contributor to morbidity and mortality. It can arise from various mechanisms including granulomatous vasculitis, lymphadenopathy causing occlusion, thrombosis, venous obstruction, vascular bed destruction, myocardial involvement leading to left heart dysfunction, portopulmonary hypertension due to liver cirrhosis, or hypoxic vasoconstriction. PH should be suspected in patients with disproportionate symptoms relative to parenchymal disease, extensive abnormalities, desaturation on a 6-min walk test, or low diffusing capacity.
keyPoints: PH is classified as WHO group 5 cause of PH
Etiologies include granulomatous vasculitis, lymphadenopathy, thrombosis, venous occlusion, vascular bed destruction, left heart dysfunction, portopulmonary hypertension, hypoxic vasoconstriction
Symptoms disproportionate to parenchymal disease, extensive abnormalities, desaturation on 6-min walk test, low diffusing capacity indicate need for PH evaluation
Echocardiography is useful for screening; right heart catheterization is the gold standard
Corticosteroids may improve pulmonary pressures in some cases
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Diagnosing Neurosarcoidosis
items.content: Neurosarcoidosis can manifest with cranial nerve involvement, particularly facial and optic nerves, often due to granulomatous basal meningitis or direct nerve involvement. MRI is preferred for evaluating brain parenchyma, meninges, dura, and spinal cord. Common MRI findings include leptomeningeal or parenchymal enhancement and white matter lesions. CSF analysis may show mononuclear cell pleocytosis, elevated proteins, and other non-diagnostic markers like elevated angiotensin-converting enzyme. Diagnosis can be established with consistent clinical features, imaging, and exclusion of other causes, sometimes requiring biopsy.
keyPoints: Cranial nerve involvement is common; facial nerve palsy has a good prognosis
MRI preferred for brain, meninges, dura, spinal cord evaluation
Common MRI findings: leptomeningeal or parenchymal enhancement, white matter lesions
CSF analysis may show mononuclear cell pleocytosis, elevated proteins; other markers are non-diagnostic
Diagnosis established with clinical features, imaging, exclusion of other causes; biopsy sometimes necessary
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Managing Pain in Sarcoidosis-Related Neuropathy
items.content: Pain is prevalent among sarcoidosis patients and may stem from neuropathic or fibromyalgia syndromes. Small-fiber neuropathy can cause pain and autonomic dysfunction, while large-fiber neuropathies are evaluated via nerve biopsy or conduction studies. Pain syndromes often do not respond well to immunosuppressive medications.
keyPoints: Pain common in sarcoidosis; may be due to neuropathic or fibromyalgia syndromes
Small-fiber neuropathy causes pain and autonomic dysfunction
Large-fiber neuropathies evaluated by nerve biopsy or conduction studies
Immunosuppressive medications often ineffective for pain syndromes
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Cutaneous Manifestations of Sarcoidosis
items.content: Sarcoidosis can present with various skin lesions, such as maculopapular, nodular eruptions, plaques, and lupus pernio. These lesions are often not painful or pruritic but may require systemic therapy if disfiguring or symptomatic.
keyPoints: Common cutaneous manifestations include granulomatous inflammation with maculopapular, nodular eruptions, and plaques.
Lupus pernio is a chronic lesion characterized by indurated plaques and violaceous discoloration, often seen in African-American females.
Nonspecific lesions like erythema nodosum are common in Europeans and women.
Systemic therapy may be needed for disfiguring or symptomatic lesions.

items.title: Importance of Ophthalmologic Evaluation in Sarcoidosis Patients
items.content: Ocular involvement in sarcoidosis can range from asymptomatic to severe, potentially leading to permanent vision loss. Uveitis is the most common ocular manifestation and requires prompt evaluation and management.
keyPoints: Ophthalmologic examination should be performed initially and with any change in visual symptoms due to potential for permanent vision loss.
Uveitis (anterior, posterior, or panuveitis) is the most common ocular manifestation and often bilateral.
Optic neuropathy can cause sudden permanent vision loss and requires emergency treatment with immunosuppressive therapy.
Biopsy of conjunctival nodules can provide diagnostic tissue in a relatively noninvasive manner.

items.title: Hepatic Granulomas in Sarcoidosis: Clinical Significance and Management
items.content: In sarcoidosis, hepatic granulomas are frequently found (up to 80% in autopsy studies) but are clinically significant only up to 15%. Liver function tests show abnormalities in about one-third of cases, with elevated alkaline phosphatase being the most common. Elevated bilirubin levels suggest more severe disease progression. Hepatomegaly is often present, and symptoms may include abdominal pain, pruritus, fevers, weight loss, and jaundice. In rare instances, hepatic sarcoidosis can lead to chronic cholestasis or cirrhosis.
keyPoints: Hepatic granulomas found in up to 80% of pulmonary sarcoidosis patients at autopsy
Clinically significant in only up to 15%
Liver function tests abnormal in up to one-third of cases
Elevated alkaline phosphatase most common lab abnormality
Elevated bilirubin indicates significant progressive disease
Hepatomegaly commonly seen
Symptoms: abdominal pain, pruritus, fevers, weight loss, jaundice
Rare progression to chronic cholestasis or cirrhosis

items.title: Musculoskeletal Manifestations of Sarcoidosis: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Sarcoidosis often presents with acute arthritis, particularly in Löfgren syndrome, which is common early in the disease. Chronic arthritis or synovitis is less frequent. The most commonly affected joints are ankles, knees, elbows, wrists, and small joints of hands and feet. Bone lesions may appear as lacy, reticular osteolytic lesions on radiographs and can be asymptomatic or painful. Symptomatic muscle involvement presents with pain, tenderness, or weakness, often resembling myositis or chronic myopathy. MRI or nuclear bone scans are useful for identifying active inflammation.
keyPoints: Acute arthritis common in early sarcoidosis (e.g., Löfgren syndrome)
Chronic arthritis or synovitis is rarer
Commonly affected joints: ankles, knees, elbows, wrists, small joints of hands and feet
Bone lesions appear as lacy, reticular osteolytic lesions on radiographs
Symptomatic muscle involvement presents with pain, tenderness, weakness
MRI or nuclear bone scans useful for identifying active inflammation

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.title: Hypercalcemia in Sarcoidosis: Mechanism and Management
items.content: In sarcoidosis, hypercalcemia occurs due to dysregulated production of calcitriol by activated macrophages within granulomas. This leads to increased calcium absorption from the gut and bone resorption, causing elevated serum calcium levels despite low parathyroid hormone levels. Untreated hypercalcemia can result in nephrocalcinosis and renal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Hypercalcemia occurs in 10–20% of sarcoidosis patients.
Hypercalciuria is seen in up to 50% of patients.
Calcitriol production by macrophages leads to increased intestinal absorption and bone resorption.
Monitor vitamin D intake, avoid excessive sunlight, maintain hydration.
Severe acute symptomatic hypercalcemia may require urgent corticosteroid therapy.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.title: Renal and Lymphatic Manifestations in Sarcoidosis
items.content: Renal dysfunction in sarcoidosis is primarily due to hypercalcemia-induced nephrocalcinosis rather than direct granulomatous infiltration. It can also be caused by interstitial nephritis, which typically responds to treatment. Peripheral lymphadenopathy may present as an initial sign of sarcoidosis, with cervical and axillary glands most commonly involved. Splenomegaly is common but usually asymptomatic.
keyPoints: Renal dysfunction often results from hypercalcemia and nephrocalcinosis.
Interstitial nephritis can occur with or without granulomas.
Urinalysis may show sterile pyuria, bland sediment, or mild proteinuria.
Peripheral lymphadenopathy involves cervical, axillary, epitrochlear, and inguinal glands.
Splenomegaly is common but typically asymptomatic.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Sarcoidosis Involvement and Prognosis in Gastrointestinal and Reproductive Organs
items.content: While gastrointestinal involvement in sarcoidosis is rare, it most commonly affects the stomach. Patients may present with symptoms like dysphagia, bleeding, nausea, vomiting, pain, weight loss, or obstructive symptoms. In reproductive organs, granulomas are more common in the female uterus and male epididymis, necessitating careful evaluation to exclude malignancy due to their rarity.
keyPoints: Gastrointestinal involvement is rare but most commonly affects the stomach.
Symptoms may include dysphagia, bleeding, nausea, vomiting, pain, weight loss, or obstructive symptoms.
Reproductive system granulomas are more common in the female uterus and male epididymis.
New nodules or masses in reproductive organs should be carefully evaluated to exclude malignancy.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.title: Clinical Course and Prognosis of Sarcoidosis
items.content: Sarcoidosis often resolves spontaneously in over half of the patients, but 30–40% will have chronic disease affecting vital organs or causing debilitating symptoms. Serious extrapulmonary involvement occurs in 4–7% at presentation and can develop later. Relapses are common with shorter treatment durations. Acute exacerbations occur in over one-third of pulmonary sarcoidosis cases. Mortality is 1–5%, primarily due to respiratory insufficiency, complications from pulmonary scarring, or organ-specific issues like cardiac involvement.
keyPoints: Over half of patients experience spontaneous resolution of sarcoidosis.
30-40% will have chronic disease affecting vital organs or causing debilitating symptoms.
Serious extrapulmonary involvement occurs in 4–7% at presentation and can develop later.
Relapses are common with shorter treatment durations.
Acute exacerbations occur in over one-third of pulmonary sarcoidosis cases.
Mortality is 1-5%, primarily due to respiratory insufficiency, complications from pulmonary scarring, or organ-specific issues like cardiac involvement.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.title: Prognostic Factors in Sarcoidosis: Identifying Favorable and Adverse Outcomes
items.content: In sarcoidosis, patients presenting with Lofgren or Heerford syndromes have a favorable prognosis, often experiencing spontaneous remission in up to 80% of cases. Conversely, certain clinical features such as lupus pernio, chronic uveitis, and cardiac involvement are associated with adverse outcomes.
keyPoints: Lofgren syndrome occurs in 20–30% of Caucasians, especially Northern Europeans, and <5% of Asians and African Americans.
Erythema nodosum and fever typically remit within 6 weeks; lymphadenopathy may take over a year to resolve.

Adverse prognostic factors include lupus pernio, chronic uveitis, older age, chronic hypercalcemia, nephrocalcinosis, African origins, pulmonary hypertension, fibrotic pulmonary sarcoidosis, sinus involvement, cystic bone lesions, neurosarcoidosis, and cardiac involvement.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.title: Utility of Biomarkers in Sarcoidosis: Serum and BAL Markers
items.content: While serum ACE levels are not highly sensitive for diagnosis or prognosis, they can be used to monitor systemic disease activity. The T-cell-derived soluble IL-2 receptor correlates with disease activity and may predict relapse rates. A high CD4:CD8 ratio in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid supports diagnosis but does not correlate with progression; higher values suggest a better prognosis.
keyPoints: Serum ACE levels are used to monitor systemic disease activity, correlating with granulomatous burden.
Soluble IL-2 receptor correlates with disease activity and may predict relapse rate.
High CD4:CD8 ratio in BAL fluid supports diagnosis and suggests a better prognosis; increased neutrophils or eosinophils in BAL indicate potential progression.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.title: Optimal Follow-Up Strategy for Sarcoidosis Patients
items.content: Intensive follow-up is crucial during the initial two years post-diagnosis of sarcoidosis, with decreasing frequency over time. Regular monitoring should include symptoms assessment, physical exams, imaging, pulmonary function tests, and organ-specific markers.
keyPoints: Follow-up most intensive in first 2 years post-presentation
Decreasing follow-up frequency as time progresses
Imaging options: chest x-rays, CT scans, PET/CT, MRI (rare cases)
Monitor for new symptoms prompting full organ evaluation

items.title: Guidelines for Treatment Initiation in Sarcoidosis
items.content: Treatment decisions should be based on symptom severity and potential organ damage. Corticosteroids are first-line, but their long-term impact is uncertain beyond 2 years. Alternative therapies may be considered for those intolerant to steroids or with refractory disease.
keyPoints: No treatment needed for asymptomatic stage I disease
Oral immunosuppressive therapy reserved for significant symptoms or organ damage
Corticosteroids improve short-term outcomes but uncertain long-term effects beyond 2 years
Consider comorbid conditions when choosing therapy

items.title: Initial Corticosteroid Dosing and Adjustment in Sarcoidosis Treatment
items.content: For sarcoidosis treatment, corticosteroids are the first-line therapy due to their efficacy. Prednisone or an equivalent is typically started at 0.5 mg/kg per day (approximately 20–40 mg/d) for 4 weeks. This dose is then reduced to a maintenance level ideally below 10 mg/d to control symptoms and disease progression, minimizing morbidity.
keyPoints: First-line therapy with corticosteroids due to efficacy and ease of use.
Initial dosing: 0.5 mg/kg per day or ~20–40 mg/d for 4 weeks.
Maintenance dose ideally below 10 mg/d.
Higher starting doses not more effective, associated with increased morbidity.

items.title: Steroid-Sparing Therapies in Sarcoidosis Management
items.content: In sarcoidosis, steroid-sparing agents are considered when corticosteroids alone are insufficient or to reduce long-term side effects. Methotrexate is the most recommended second-line therapy due to its efficacy and favorable side effect profile. Azathioprine is also effective but may be chosen based on individual patient factors.
keyPoints: Steroid-sparing agents used when corticosteroids are insufficient or for long-term management.
Methotrexate is the most recommended second-line therapy due to efficacy and favorable side effect profile.
Azathioprine is equally effective but choice depends on patient factors like alcohol use, pregnancy plans, pharmacogenetic profiles, and comorbidities.

items.question: Which population has the highest reported incidence of sarcoidosis?
options: U.S. Black Women's Health Study cohort
items.explanation: The highest incidence is in African Americans at 35.5 per 100,000.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.question: Which gender generally manifests sarcoidosis earlier?
options: Both manifest at the same age
items.explanation: Men tend to manifest disease earlier than women.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.question: What is the peak incidence age range for sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Peak incidence occurs between 35 and 45 years of age.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.question: Which factor is associated with reduced odds of incident sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Smoking is associated with reduced odds of incident sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.question: What is the leading cause of death in sarcoidosis patients according to the passage?
items.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis is identified as the leading cause of death in sarcoidosis patients.

items.question: Which population has a higher mortality rate from sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Mortality rates are noted to be higher in women.

items.question: What is the average age- and gender-adjusted mortality rate of sarcoidosis in the United States?
items.explanation: The passage states an average age- and gender-adjusted mortality rate of 4.32 per 1,000,000.

items.question: In which country is the mortality from sarcoidosis usually attributed to cardiac involvement?
items.explanation: The passage notes that in Japan, mortality from sarcoidosis is usually due to cardiac involvement.

items.question: Which type of immune response is the immunopathogenic hallmark of sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The immunopathogenic hallmark of sarcoidosis is characterized by highly polarized CD4+ T_H1 lymphocytes.

items.question: What syndrome associated with sarcoidosis includes fever, erythema nodosum, ankle arthralgias, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy?
items.explanation: Löfgren syndrome is associated with these symptoms and seen in up to 10% of sarcoidosis cases.

items.question: Which cytokine is prominently produced by activated lymphocytes in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Activated lymphocytes in sarcoidosis produce interferon-gamma (IFN-γ), characteristic of T_H1 immune polarization.

items.question: Which diagnostic method has a high yield and low procedural risk for diagnosing sarcoidosis with prominent lung involvement?
items.explanation: Endobronchial ultrasound-guided lymph node sampling is preferred for its high diagnostic yield and low procedural risk.

items.question: Which of the following conditions might allow deferral of biopsy in sarcoidosis diagnosis?
items.explanation: Biopsy may be deferred in patients with classic Löfgren syndrome due to its compelling clinical presentation.

items.question: What is the significance of a CD4:CD8 lymphocyte ratio >3.5 in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) for sarcoidosis diagnosis?
options: It confirms the presence of infection.
It supports a diagnosis of sarcoidosis, especially with consistent clinical and radiographic findings.
It indicates a need for surgical lung biopsy.
items.explanation: A CD4:CD8 ratio >3.5 supports a diagnosis of sarcoidosis when combined with consistent clinical and radiographic findings.

items.question: Which of the following cell types is NOT typically found within the center of a sarcoidosis granuloma?
items.explanation: CD8+ T lymphocytes reside at the periphery of sarcoidosis granulomas, not within the center.

items.question: What histologic feature strongly suggests an alternative diagnosis to sarcoidosis when found in granulomatous inflammation?
items.explanation: More abundant necrosis strongly indicates alternative causes of granulomatous inflammation such as infection.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical inclusion found in sarcoidosis granulomas?
items.explanation: Fibrin thrombi are not mentioned as typical inclusions in sarcoidosis granulomas.

items.question: Which pattern of pulmonary involvement is often seen in patients with more advanced sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: A restrictive pattern is often seen in those with more advanced sarcoidosis.

items.question: What is the typical distribution of sarcoidosis granulomas in the lung?
options: Randomly distributed throughout the lung parenchyma
Along the pleural and subpleural areas, interlobular septa, and around bronchovascular bundles
Concentrated at the apex of the lungs
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis granulomas have a lymphangitic distribution in the lung along the pleural and subpleural areas, interlobular septa, and around bronchovascular bundles.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a classic finding on chest CT scans for sarcoidosis?
options: Widespread small nodules with a bronchovascular distribution
items.explanation: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage is not a classic finding on chest CT scans for sarcoidosis.

items.question: Which imaging technique is currently preferred for diagnosing cardiac sarcoidosis due to its superior resolution?
options: Technetium-sestamibi single-photon emission CT nuclear scans
items.explanation: Enhanced cardiac MRI is preferred due to its superior resolution and ability to detect early myocardial involvement with high sensitivity and specificity.

items.question: What is the most common cause of death among patients with cardiac sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Sudden death is the most common cause of death among cardiac sarcoidosis patients.

items.question: Which ECG finding should prompt further cardiac evaluation in a patient with sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Symptoms or unexpected ECG findings such as ST-T wave abnormalities should prompt further cardiac evaluation.

items.question: What is a predictor of sudden death in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Predictors of sudden death include severity of heart failure, among other factors.

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of pulmonary hypertension in sarcoidosis?
options: Hypoxic vasoconstriction related to parenchymal abnormalities
Left heart dysfunction due to myocardial involvement
items.explanation: Primary biliary cirrhosis is not listed as a cause of pulmonary hypertension in sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary hypertension?
items.explanation: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.question: Which cranial nerve involvement in sarcoidosis has a good prognosis?
items.explanation: Facial nerve palsy is associated with a good prognosis compared to other neurologic findings.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.question: Which imaging modality is preferred for evaluating brain parenchyma in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI is the preferred test for evaluating brain parenchyma in sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.question: Which of the following skin lesions is most commonly associated with sarcoidosis in Europeans and women?
items.explanation: Nonspecific, nongranulomatous lesions of erythema nodosum are a well-established manifestation of sarcoidosis in Europeans and women.

items.question: Which ocular condition associated with sarcoidosis can lead to sudden permanent vision loss?
items.explanation: Optic neuropathy can lead to sudden permanent vision loss and should be treated as an emergency.

items.question: Which of the following treatments is recommended for disfiguring or symptomatic skin lesions in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Disfiguring or symptomatic skin lesions in sarcoidosis may need systemic therapy.

items.question: Which laboratory finding is most commonly abnormal in sarcoidosis cases involving the liver?
items.explanation: Elevated alkaline phosphatase is the most common lab abnormality in sarcoidosis cases involving the liver.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is NOT commonly associated with hepatic sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Nephrolithiasis is not a symptom of hepatic sarcoidosis; abdominal pain, pruritus, and jaundice are.

items.question: What imaging finding is most commonly associated with sarcoidosis involving the liver?
items.explanation: Imaging of the liver often shows hepatomegaly in cases of sarcoidosis.

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.question: Which of the following is a common consequence of untreated hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis?
options: Pressure or pain from splenic involvement
items.explanation: Untreated hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis leads to nephrocalcinosis and subsequent renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.question: What is the primary cause of hypercalcemia in patients with sarcoidosis?
options: Dysregulated production of calcitriol by activated macrophages
items.explanation: Hypercalcemia in sarcoidosis is primarily due to dysregulated production of calcitriol by activated macrophages.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.question: Which renal condition associated with sarcoidosis is generally responsive to treatment?
options: Interstitial nephritis with or without granulomas
Renal dysfunction from excessive sunlight exposure
items.explanation: Interstitial nephritis associated with sarcoidosis is generally responsive to treatment.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.question: Which part of the gastrointestinal tract is most commonly involved in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The stomach is the most common site of gastrointestinal involvement in sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.question: In which reproductive organ are granulomas most commonly found in males with sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Granulomas most commonly appear within the male epididymis in cases of sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.question: What percentage of sarcoidosis patients have chronic disease that threatens vital organs or causes debilitating symptoms?
items.explanation: A significant proportion, specifically 30–40%, of sarcoidosis patients will have chronic disease that threatens vital organs or causes debilitating symptoms.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.question: Which clinical syndrome associated with sarcoidosis is characterized by an excellent prognosis and spontaneous remission in up to 80% of cases?
items.explanation: Lofgren syndrome is associated with an excellent prognosis and spontaneous remission in up to 80% of cases.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.question: Which factor is NOT considered an adverse clinical prognostic factor in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Erythema nodosum is associated with a good prognosis, not an adverse one.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.question: Which of the following markers lacks sensitivity for diagnosis but correlates with disease activity in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The T-cell-derived soluble IL-2 receptor lacks sensitivity for diagnosis but correlates with disease activity.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.question: During which period should the follow-up and monitoring of sarcoidosis be most intensive?
options: Once in full remission for a few years
items.explanation: Follow-up should be most intensive during the initial 2-year period after diagnosis.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an indication for starting treatment in sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: Treatment is not indicated for asymptomatic stage I sarcoidosis due to its high rate of spontaneous resolution.

items.question: What should be considered when choosing a therapy for sarcoidosis?
options: Potential benefits versus risks and toxicity of treatment
items.explanation: Therapy choice should balance potential benefits against risks and toxicity, including comorbid conditions.

items.question: What is the initial recommended daily dose of prednisone for treating sarcoidosis?
items.explanation: The initial recommended dose is generally 0.5 mg/kg per day or approximately 20-40 mg/day for 4 weeks.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason to consider higher doses of corticosteroids in sarcoidosis treatment?
options: Ocular involvement leading to vision loss
items.explanation: Higher doses are considered for life- or organ-threatening situations, not mild pulmonary symptoms.

items.question: What is the most recommended second-line therapy in sarcoidosis due to its efficacy and favorable side effect profile?
items.explanation: Methotrexate is the most recommended second-line therapy due to its efficacy and favorable side effect profile.

items.statement: The incidence and prevalence of sarcoidosis are consistent across all races, genders, and geographic regions.
items.explanation: Incidence and prevalence vary by race, gender, geographic region, ethnicity, and season.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.statement: Cardiac involvement is more commonly observed in European populations.
items.explanation: Cardiac involvement is more common in Japanese populations.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.statement: Sarcoidosis has a known single cause.
items.explanation: The etiology of sarcoidosis is unknown, with evidence pointing to multiple potential environmental exposures.

items.statement: Sarcoidosis mortality rates are higher in African Americans compared to other racial groups.
items.explanation: The passage states that mortality rates are higher in African Americans.

items.statement: The critical step as to why some patients with sarcoidosis transition to fibrosis is well understood.
items.explanation: The passage states that the reason for the transition from sarcoidosis to fibrosis is not understood.

items.statement: Löfgren syndrome typically has a poor prognosis with spontaneous resolution.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that Löfgren syndrome usually has an excellent prognosis with full spontaneous resolution.

items.statement: The presence of well-established, clinically useful diagnostic serum biomarkers is crucial for diagnosing sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: There are no well-established, clinically useful diagnostic serum biomarkers that can secure a diagnosis of sarcoidosis.

items.statement: A normal CD4:CD8 lymphocyte ratio in BAL excludes the diagnosis of sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: A normal CD4:CD8 ratio does not exclude sarcoidosis; it is one supportive finding among others.

items.statement: Sarcoidosis granulomas typically exhibit abundant necrosis.
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis granulomas are nonnecrotizing, with punctuate necrosis being rare.

items.statement: A baseline ophthalmologic investigation is recommended for all sarcoidosis patients to evaluate for clinically silent uveitis.
items.explanation: Baseline ophthalmologic investigation is part of the initial evaluation for sarcoidosis.

items.statement: Sarcoidosis tends to be lower-lobe predominant on lung imaging.
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis tends to be upper-lobe predominant on lung imaging.

items.statement: Pleural effusions in sarcoidosis are usually lymphocytic and exudative.
items.explanation: Pleural effusions tend to be indirectly related (e.g., infections, heart failure), but rarely, sarcoidosis can cause lymphocytic, exudative pleural effusions.

items.statement: The presence of pulmonary involvement predicts cardiac sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Severity of pulmonary involvement does not predict the presence of cardiac sarcoidosis.

items.statement: Cardiac PET/CT can be used to detect active inflammation when cardiac MRI is inconclusive.
items.explanation: Cardiac PET/CT can detect active inflammation in cases where cardiac MRI is unavailable or inconclusive.

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.statement: Corticosteroids always improve pulmonary pressures in cases of granulomatous disease.
items.explanation: Treating granulomatous disease with corticosteroids may result in improvement of pulmonary pressures, but not in all cases.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.statement: Peripheral neuropathy can be evaluated by nerve biopsy or nerve conduction studies.
items.explanation: Large-fiber neuropathies in sarcoidosis can be evaluated using nerve biopsy or nerve conduction studies.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.statement: Lupus pernio is most commonly found among members of the African-American female population.
items.explanation: Lupus pernio appears most commonly among members of the African-American female population.

items.statement: Conjunctival nodules in sarcoidosis are best diagnosed with a biopsy.
items.explanation: If conjunctival nodules are present, biopsy can be a relatively noninvasive way to obtain diagnostic tissue.

items.statement: Bone marrow involvement in sarcoidosis can cause hematologic abnormalities such as anemia and lymphopenia.
items.explanation: The passage states that bone marrow involvement can lead to hematologic abnormalities like anemia and lymphopenia.

items.statement: Acute arthritis in sarcoidosis is more common than chronic arthritis or synovitis.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that acute arthritis, such as seen in Löfgren syndrome, is common early in the disease course.

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.statement: Peripheral lymphadenopathy in sarcoidosis often presents with ulceration or drainage.
items.explanation: Enlarged glands in sarcoidosis are discrete, movable, and nontender without ulceration or drainage.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS
items.statement: Parotid gland involvement in sarcoidosis is often associated with Heerfordt syndrome.
items.explanation: Parotid or salivary involvement can be part of Heerfordt syndrome, which includes parotitis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · HYPERCALCEMIA | KIDNEY | LYMPHOID SYSTEM | PAROTID GLANDS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.statement: Serious extrapulmonary involvement occurs in more than 10% of sarcoidosis patients at presentation.
items.explanation: Serious extrapulmonary involvement occurs in 4–7% of sarcoidosis patients at presentation.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS
items.statement: Most cases of sarcoidosis show improvement in chest radiography or spirometry after 2 years.
items.explanation: Improvement is seen in most cases of sarcoidosis, including treated and untreated, in chest radiography or spirometry after 2 years.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT | REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS | CLINICAL COURSE AND PROGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.statement: Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) levels are highly useful as a diagnostic tool for sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Serum ACE levels have low sensitivity and are not largely useful as a diagnostic tool.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.subtopic: PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS
items.statement: Increased percentages of BAL neutrophils or eosinophils are associated with a better prognosis in sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Increased percentages of BAL neutrophils or eosinophils are present in individuals with progression, indicating a worse prognosis.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · CLINICAL FACTORS OF PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE | PREDICTIVE VALUE OF SERUM, BRONCHOALVEOLAR LAVAGE, AND GENETICS

items.statement: Corticosteroids are the only treatment option for sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: While corticosteroids are first-line, other immunosuppressive therapies may be used if needed.

items.statement: Sarcoidosis can recur in transplanted organs but rarely causes organ failure post-transplant.
items.explanation: Recurrence is possible after transplantation, but it seldom leads to organ failure.

items.statement: Inhaled corticosteroids have been shown to be of benefit in the larger sarcoidosis population.
items.explanation: Inhaled corticosteroids may be considered for symptom control but are not proven beneficial in the larger sarcoidosis population.

items.statement: Higher starting doses of corticosteroids are largely effective and do not increase morbidity in most cases of sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Higher starting doses are generally not more effective and are associated with increased morbidity.

items.assertion: The overall severity of sarcoidosis is higher in black populations.
items.reason: Black patients present with more frequent involvement of extrapulmonary disease and a three times greater likelihood of familial disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.assertion: Smoking is a risk factor for developing sarcoidosis.
items.reason: Smoking is associated with reduced odds of incident sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because smoking reduces the odds, not increases the risk.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · Sarcoidosis | EPIDEMIOLOGY | INCIDENCE AND PREVALENCE

items.assertion: Sarcoidosis is associated with genetic predisposition.
items.reason: Antigen recognition and presentation molecule polymorphisms have been linked to sarcoidosis development.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Smoking increases the risk of developing sarcoidosis.
items.reason: Nicotine has immunomodulatory effects that may protect against sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; smoking is a negative predictor, while the reason correctly explains why.

items.reason: Granulomas infiltrating the heart cause rhythm abnormalities and other complications.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why isolated cardiac sarcoidosis is rare.

items.assertion: Every patient with sarcoidosis should be queried for potential cardiac symptoms.
items.reason: Cardiac involvement can have life-threatening complications and may occur without pulmonary involvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients should be queried.

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.assertion: Sarcoidosis affecting the nervous system can occur without significant pulmonary or systemic disease.
items.reason: Cranial nerve involvement is highly associated with systemic sarcoidosis but can also be seen in isolated cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.subtopic: PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT
items.assertion: Histologic confirmation is always necessary for diagnosing neurosarcoidosis.
items.reason: Diagnosis can often be established by biopsy of a nonneurologic site with consistent clinical features and imaging.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because histologic confirmation in the brain or spinal cord is only occasionally necessary.
items.reference: Sarcoidosis · PULMONARY ARTERY HYPERTENSION | NERVOUS SYSTEM INVOLVEMENT

items.assertion: Methotrexate is the most recommended second-line therapy in sarcoidosis.
items.reason: It has a favorable side effect profile and proven efficacy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why methotrexate is preferred.
